claimed_contribution: Introduces social versus solitary framing of frequent indulgences as a key predictor of whether consumers prefer to cut frequency or cut intensity.
Shows that anticipated enjoyment has separable sources (the product itself vs. aspects outside the product) and that these sources differentially drive cutback preferences.
Provides convergent evidence across multiple paradigms, including real-choice studies, a two-week follow-up dieting study, and a controlled Game of Life style simulation of repeated consumption.
context: The paper examines recurring hedonic indulgences (for example, coffee drinks and indulgent foods) where consumers aim to reduce monetary spending or caloric intake. Empirical settings include U.S. consumers in budgeting and dieting contexts, a lab simulation of weekly coffeehouse behavior, and scenario-based app decisions.
design_and_data: The article reports six experiments. Experiment 1 (MTurk, N=598 eligible participants) used an incentive-compatible choice of a financial guide focused on cutting frequency versus cutting intensity; Experiment 2 (MTurk, N=600 in part 1, 477 in part 2) used a two-week dieting-plan choice linked to access to a corresponding advice website and follow-up reports. Experiment 3 (lab undergraduates, final N=264 after allergy exclusions) used a four-week Game of Life simulation with real edible choices and a consequential midstream cutback decision. Experiments 4-6 (Prolific, N=452, 451, 452) used mixed designs that separately measured interest in cutting frequency and cutting intensity, measured anticipated enjoyment from product versus nonproduct aspects, and tested moderators (friends assurance, close vs. distant others, and a solitary enjoyment intervention).
focal_constructs: anticipated enjoyment from aspects outside the product
main_findings: Across studies, framing an indulgence as social rather than solitary decreased preference for cutting frequency and increased preference for cutting intensity.

When measured separately, social framing reduced interest in frequency-cutting options and increased interest in intensity-cutting options, and this pattern was mediated by greater anticipated enjoyment from aspects outside the product (with additional mediation via product enjoyment in some analyses).

The social-solitary divergence was eliminated for social experiences with distant others and for solitary experiences reframed to emphasize enjoyment outside the product, while it persisted even when friends assured equal opportunities to socialize if frequency were cut.
one_line_summary: Across six experiments, thinking of a frequent indulgence as social rather than solitary shifted consumers away from cutting frequency and toward cutting intensity, largely because social framing heightens anticipated enjoyment from nonproduct aspects of the experience.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior consumer goal and resource expenditure work by comparing two moderation routes to indulging less (cutting frequency vs. within-category intensity) rather than mainly indulgence-versus-abstinence or solitary substitution choices. extends social-versus-solitary consumption research beyond enjoyment and closeness outcomes by linking social context to cutback strategy preferences through distinct anticipated enjoyment sources.
research_gap: Prior social-versus-solitary research mainly examined how social consumption changes enjoyment, not how it shapes preferred ways to cut back. Prior cutback research largely emphasized indulgence versus nonindulgence or substitution decisions in solitary contexts and did not directly compare frequency reduction with within-category intensity reduction as a function of social context.
research_question: When consumers want or need to cut back on a frequent indulgence, does thinking of that indulgence as social versus solitary change whether they prefer reducing frequency or reducing intensity? The paper also asks why this pattern occurs, focusing on anticipated enjoyment from the product itself versus from aspects outside the product, and when the effect weakens.
